case_name: New Balance Athletics Inc. vs Ashok Kumar & Ors.
court: High Court of Delhi at New Delhi

compensation_breakdown.tribunal_amount: Rs. 11,02,460/- (claimed in Affidavit of Cost dated 21.11.2024, includes Court fees Rs. 2,01,000/- and Local Commissioner's fee Rs. 1,00,000/-)
compensation_breakdown.notes: Actual litigation costs and related expenses awarded as per the High Court order.

facts: Plaintiff New Balance Athletics Inc. sued defendants for trademark infringement, passing off and unfair competition arising from the sale of counterfeit footwear on the website www.luxurytag.in. Plaintiff alleged long-standing use and registrations of the NEW BALANCE and NB marks in India and worldwide and that defendant nos. 1 and 11 operated the impugned website selling 'high quality products which look similar to the originals'. A representative of the plaintiff purchased a product in February 2023 from the website and identified it as counterfeit. Defendant nos. 1 and 11 failed to appear or file a written statement; other defendants were proforma or had complied with court directions.
arguments_claimant: The plaintiff argued that its registered NEW BALANCE marks have been used in India since 1986 and enjoy substantial goodwill, that the impugned website offered counterfeit footwear bearing those marks, and that screenshots and an actual purchase established infringement and passing off. The plaintiff sought permanent injunctions, rendition of accounts, delivery up, freezing of assets and actual litigation costs (Affidavit of Cost claiming Rs. 11,02,460/-). The plaintiff relied on the triple identity test and Section 29(2)(c) read with 29(3) of the Trade Marks Act to establish a presumption of likelihood of confusion.
arguments_respondent: Defendant nos. 1 and 11 did not appear or file written statements and therefore did not advance substantive legal arguments; the website itself contained admissions that the products sold were not originals. Other named defendants either complied with court directions or were treated as proforma defendants; some defendants provided information which enabled impleadment and service.
ratio_decidendi: "The triple identity test, which envisages identity of marks, goods and consumers and availability of the goods through identical sources and outlets, is satisfied" and the facts "directly invoke Section 29 (2)(c) read with 29(3) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, under which, where the rival marks are identical, and used in respect of identical goods or services, the Court would presume the likelihood of confusion in the minds of the consumer." The court applied these principles to the present facts because the impugned website sold goods bearing identical marks to the plaintiff's registered trademarks, targeted the same consumer base, and the defendants' own website statements admitted sale of replica/fake/counterfeit products. The court further recognised that "counterfeiting is a serious economic offence" that "has to be dealt with strictly and no leniency can be shown," and held that where counterfeiting and infringement are clearly made out the plaintiff is entitled to actual litigation costs; accordingly the suit was decreed and actual costs awarded.
final_order: The suit is decreed in favour of the plaintiff and against defendant nos. 1 and 11 in terms of paragraph 68(a), (b), (c) and (e) of the plaint; plaintiff is entitled to actual costs amounting to Rs. 14,51,670/-, payable by defendant nos. 1 and 11 jointly and severally within eight weeks. Registry directed to draw up the decree sheet and the suit along with pending application stands disposed of.
summary: This is a trademark infringement and passing off suit by New Balance Athletics Inc. against operators of the website www.luxurytag.in for sale of counterfeit footwear. The High Court found that the triple identity test and Section 29(2)(c) read with 29(3) applied, noting the defendants' website admissions of selling replicas and the targeting of the same consumers. The court granted a decree for the plaintiff, awarded actual litigation costs of Rs. 14,51,670/- and directed the decree be drawn. The decision underscores strict treatment of online counterfeiting and the availability of actual costs where infringement is clear.
embedding_summary: New Balance Athletics Inc. v. Ashok Kumar & Ors., High Court of Delhi, CS(COMM) 104/2023 & I.A. 3739/2023, decided 27.11.2024. Plaintiff alleged sale of counterfeit NEW BALANCE footwear on www.luxurytag.in (rogue e-commerce website), purchased a sample in Feb 2023 and established counterfeit; defendants 1 and 11 admitted on the website that they "don’t sell Original products". Court applied the "triple identity test" and held that facts "directly invoke Section 29 (2)(c) read with 29(3) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999" creating a presumption of likelihood of confusion; cited Puma SE v. Brijendra Singh (2023 SCC OnLine Del 6690) and FAO (OS)(COMM) 65/2023 (Jawed Ansari v. Louis Vuitton Malletier) on strict approach to counterfeiting. Suit decreed for plaintiff; actual costs of Rs. 14,51,670/- awarded (payable jointly and severally by defendant nos. 1 and 11). Key searchable terms: New Balance, luxurytag.in, counterfeit, triple identity test, Section 29(2)(c), Section 29(3), actual costs, Delhi High Court, 27-11-2024.
legal_principles: Triple identity test satisfied where marks, goods and consumers are identical for infringement/passing off
Section 29(2)(c) read with Section 29(3) Trade Marks Act, 1999 gives presumption of likelihood of confusion where rival marks are identical on identical goods
Counterfeiting is a serious economic offence warranting strict measures and no leniency
Admissions on a defendant's own website (e.g., 'we don’t sell Original products') can constitute prima facie evidence of infringement and passing off
Court may award actual litigation costs (including local commissioner fees) where counterfeiting and infringement are clearly made out
Where contesting defendants fail to file written statement, pleadings in plaint stand admitted under Order VIII Rule 10 CPC
cases_cited: Puma SE Versus Brijendra Singh trading AS Sastajoota, 2023 SCC OnLine Del 6690
Jawed Ansari v. Louis Vuitton Malletier, FAO (OS)(COMM) 65/2023
bm25_text: New Balance Athletics Inc. vs Ashok Kumar & Ors. High Court of Delhi at New Delhi 2024 Plaintiff New Balance Athletics Inc. sued defendants for trademark infringement, passing off and unfair competition arising from the sale of counterfeit footwear on the website www.luxurytag.in. Plaintiff alleged long-standing use and registrations of the NEW BALANCE and NB marks in India and worldwide and that defendant nos. 1 and 11 operated the impugned website selling 'high quality products which look similar to the originals'. A representative of the plaintiff purchased a product in February 2023 from the website and identified it as counterfeit. Defendant nos. 1 and 11 failed to appear or file a written statement; other defendants were proforma or had complied with court directions. The plaintiff argued that its registered NEW BALANCE marks have been used in India since 1986 and enjoy substantial goodwill, that the impugned website offered counterfeit footwear bearing those marks, and that screenshots and an actual purchase established infringement and passing off. The plaintiff sought permanent injunctions, rendition of accounts, delivery up, freezing of assets and actual litigation costs (Affidavit of Cost claiming Rs. 11,02,460/-). The plaintiff relied on the triple identity test and Section 29(2)(c) read with 29(3) of the Trade Marks Act to establish a presumption of likelihood of confusion. Defendant nos. 1 and 11 did not appear or file written statements and therefore did not advance substantive legal arguments; the website itself contained admissions that the products sold were not originals. Other named defendants either complied with court directions or were treated as proforma defendants; some defendants provided information which enabled impleadment and service. "The triple identity test, which envisages identity of marks, goods and consumers and availability of the goods through identical sources and outlets, is satisfied" and the facts "directly invoke Section 29 (2)(c) read with 29(3) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999, under which, where the rival marks are identical, and used in respect of identical goods or services, the Court would presume the likelihood of confusion in the minds of the consumer." The court applied these principles to the present facts because the impugned website sold goods bearing identical marks to the plaintiff's registered trademarks, targeted the same consumer base, and the defendants' own website statements admitted sale of replica/fake/counterfeit products. The court further recognised that "counterfeiting is a serious economic offence" that "has to be dealt with strictly and no leniency can be shown," and held that where counterfeiting and infringement are clearly made out the plaintiff is entitled to actual litigation costs; accordingly the suit was decreed and actual costs awarded. The suit is decreed in favour of the plaintiff and against defendant nos. 1 and 11 in terms of paragraph 68(a), (b), (c) and (e) of the plaint; plaintiff is entitled to actual costs amounting to Rs. 14,51,670/-, payable by defendant nos. 1 and 11 jointly and severally within eight weeks. Registry directed to draw up the decree sheet and the suit along with pending application stands disposed of. This is a trademark infringement and passing off suit by New Balance Athletics Inc. against operators of the website www.luxurytag.in for sale of counterfeit footwear. The High Court found that the triple identity test and Section 29(2)(c) read with 29(3) applied, noting the defendants' website admissions of selling replicas and the targeting of the same consumers. The court granted a decree for the plaintiff, awarded actual litigation costs of Rs. 14,51,670/- and directed the decree be drawn. The decision underscores strict treatment of online counterfeiting and the availability of actual costs where infringement is clear. New Balance Athletics Inc. v. Ashok Kumar & Ors., High Court of Delhi, CS(COMM) 104/2023 & I.A. 3739/2023, decided 27.11.2024. Plaintiff alleged sale of counterfeit NEW BALANCE footwear on www.luxurytag.in (rogue e-commerce website), purchased a sample in Feb 2023 and established counterfeit; defendants 1 and 11 admitted on the website that they "don’t sell Original products". Court applied the "triple identity test" and held that facts "directly invoke Section 29 (2)(c) read with 29(3) of the Trade Marks Act, 1999" creating a presumption of likelihood of confusion; cited Puma SE v. Brijendra Singh (2023 SCC OnLine Del 6690) and FAO (OS)(COMM) 65/2023 (Jawed Ansari v. Louis Vuitton Malletier) on strict approach to counterfeiting. Suit decreed for plaintiff; actual costs of Rs. 14,51,670/- awarded (payable jointly and severally by defendant nos. 1 and 11).
